response:
"Graph theory and linear algebra provide a powerful framework for modeling and analyzing complex networks. The adjacency matrix $A$ captures the structure of the graph with its entries. Each entry $A_{ij}$ reflects the strength of the connection from node $i$ to node $j$.

* * *

The matrix exponential $e^A$ extends the adjacency matrix to model continuous-time processes. It aggregates all possible paths between nodes weighted by their probabilities. The element $ (e^A)_{ij} $ sums over all paths from $i$ to $j$ in a probabilistic manner.

* * *

SVD decomposes $A$ into singular vectors and values for efficient approximation. The rank-reduction technique retains the most significant components of $A$. This minimizes the Frobenius norm difference between $A$ and its approximation $B_k$. The optimal $B_k$ is constructed from the top $k$ singular values and vectors."
